app.name: Zoned
app.tagline: 200+ running workouts, strength training, 5K to marathon plans, 9 calculators and Garmin export. Free, no account.

data.confirmDescription: Choose how to apply the backup before restoring your Zoned data.
data.replaceModeDescription: Clears local Zoned data first, then restores only what is present in the backup.
data.mergeModeDescription: Keeps local Zoned data missing from the backup and only replaces keys present in the file.

onboarding.step1Desc: Recovery, endurance, threshold, VO2max, hills, fartlek... Filter by duration, difficulty or zone. All free, no account needed.

onboarding.step3Desc: Generate a plan from 5K to marathon or build your own freely. Export to Garmin, calendar or PDF.

onboarding.quizHint: 5 questions to find the perfect workout

articleFaq.q1: My watch shows Zone 4 but I feel like I'm in Zone 2. Who should I trust?
articleFaq.a1: Trust your sensations. Heart rate can be skewed by heat, stress, fatigue, caffeine, and sensor position. The golden rule for Zone 2: if you can hold a normal conversation without effort, you're in the right zone regardless of what your watch displays.
articleFaq.q2: Should I stay exactly in my zone?
articleFaq.a2: No, zones are indicative ranges, not beat-precise targets. Being 3-5 bpm above or below the limit has no significant physiological importance. What matters is respecting the session's objective.
articleFaq.q3: How many times per week should I run?
articleFaq.a3: Beginners (< 1 year): 2-3 outings per week. Regular runners: 3-5 outings covering most goals from 10K to marathon, alternating hard and easy days. Advanced runners: 5-7 outings, sometimes doubles, requiring years of gradual progression.
articleFaq.q4: Is the 10% rule a myth or reality?
articleFaq.a4: The 10% rule (don't increase weekly volume by more than 10%) is a useful guardrail, not an absolute physiological law. It forces progressivity, but doesn't account for training history. Use it as a guiding principle, not a rigid rule.
articleFaq.a5: First cardiovascular changes appear as early as 2-3 weeks of regular training. Measurable progress on a test or race generally arrives after 6-12 weeks of consistent work. Patience is crucial - changing programs every 3 weeks never lets adaptations settle.
articleFaq.q6: I'm in pain during the run. Continue or stop?
articleFaq.a6: Distinguish discomfort from pain. Discomfort (heaviness, slight stiffness, soreness) is often normal and usually passes after 10-15 minutes. Pain (sharp, localized, increasing or causing limping): stop immediately. When in doubt, a session missed out of caution is always preferable to weeks of forced rest.
articleFaq.q7: How do I prevent running injuries?
articleFaq.a7: Progress gradually (most injuries come from increasing volume or intensity too quickly). Vary stimuli (different terrains, shoes in rotation, different paces). Do strength training 15-20 minutes twice a week for hamstrings, quads, calves and glutes. Listen to warning signals early.
articleFaq.q8: Morning or evening, what's better for running?
articleFaq.a8: Physiologically, evening has a slight advantage: higher body temperature, more mobile joints, better reaction time. But the best time is when you actually run. Consistency beats theoretical optimization.
articleFaq.a9: It's possible but with precautions: alternate easy and hard days, keep at least 1-2 days per week very light or cross-training (cycling, swimming), and listen to your body. Many runners thrive with 5-6 days per week and 1-2 days of complete rest. Rest is part of training.
